name: Alunite Ridge
short_description: BLM rockhounding site in central Utah featuring quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Free public access with 25 lb daily collection limit.
description:
Alunite Ridge is a public BLM rockhounding area in Piute County, Utah, offering accessible mineral collecting for enthusiasts of all skill levels. The site is named for the alunite mineral deposits that characterize the surrounding hydrothermal alteration zone, though collectors also find quality quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens throughout the ridge and adjacent washes.

The geology of Alunite Ridge reflects the complex mineralization associated with the Piute Mining District, where mid-Tertiary volcanic and hydrothermal activity created a favourable environment for aluminous and silicate mineral formation. The ridge sits within a broader zone of acid sulfate alteration typical of epithermal mineralization events that occurred millions of years ago, resulting in the diverse mineral assemblages visible today.

This location appeals to both beginners and intermediate collectors. The open terrain and exposed mineral outcrops make it easy to spot specimens without extensive digging. Most productive collecting occurs along the ridge shoulders and in erosional washes where weathering naturally exposes fresh mineral faces. Quartz veining is particularly prominent, often displaying nice clarity and size.

Access is straightforward via BLM public land with no permit required. The area is relatively unvisited compared to major Utah rockhounding destinations, which means less competition for finds and a quieter experience. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting weather, though the site is usable year-round in suitable conditions.
history: Alunite Ridge sits within the Piute Mining District, where mid-Tertiary volcanic and hydrothermal activity (roughly 25-35 million years ago) created intense acid-sulfate alteration that deposited alunite, quartz, and feldspar minerals in veins and replacement zones. Early prospectors worked this region for precious metals associated with the same hydrothermal systems, leaving the mineral-rich landscape that rockhounders explore today.
tips: Search the ridge shoulders and exposed outcrops where weathering has naturally exposed quartz veins and feldspar-rich zones. Bring a small sledgehammer or pry bar to carefully separate specimens from the host rock, and look for translucent to milky quartz chunks showing clear terminations. The erosional washes running downslope from the ridge crest concentrate loose specimens; work these areas first before attempting outcrop removal.
terrain_notes: Rolling high-desert terrain at roughly 8,500 feet elevation with sparse pinyon-juniper woodland and native brush. The ridge itself is a moderate slope (15-25 degrees) with mixed rocky footing and loose talus. Parking is typically found at natural pullouts; walking distance to productive collecting areas ranges from 100 feet to half a mile depending on specific search focus. Footing is rocky but not extremely technical; sturdy hiking boots are recommended.
written_directions: From Junction (the nearest town), head north on US 89 for approximately 18 miles toward Salina. Look for Piute County Road 17 (sometimes signed as a BLM access road) branching east. Follow this road approximately 12 miles east as it climbs toward the higher elevations. Alunite Ridge will be on your right (south); look for natural dirt pullouts along the road near exposed mineral outcrops and ridge shoulders. High-clearance vehicle recommended but passenger cars may access in dry conditions.
road_conditions: Dirt and gravel BLM access road, washboard surface common. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Passable in dry weather; avoid during or immediately after rain.
parking_notes: Informal dirt pullouts along the access road near mineral outcrops. Capacity varies by location; typically room for 2-4 vehicles. No developed facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy machinery. Collecting must not disturb sensitive archaeological or botanical resources. Follow Leave No Trace principles.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or heavy machinery
No disturbance of archaeological sites or protected plants
Follow Leave No Trace principles
nearest_services: Junction, Utah, approximately 30 miles southwest. Limited services; gas and basic supplies available. Salina, approximately 20 miles north, offers more comprehensive services including restaurants and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy access for most hikers. Terrain is rocky with moderate slope but no steep cliffs or technical scrambling. Uneven footing and loose talus require sturdy hiking boots. No wheelchair accessibility. Most productive collecting areas are within easy walking distance of pullouts.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Alunite Ridge in Utah?
faq.answer: Alunite Ridge is primarily known for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. The site takes its name from alunite deposits associated with historic hydrothermal mineralization. You may also encounter other silicate minerals common to the Piute Mining District. Most collectors focus on quartz veining and feldspar-rich outcrops.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Alunite Ridge, or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Alunite Ridge is completely free with no permit required. The site is on BLM public land governed by recreational collecting rules. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use only, with no commercial collection allowed.

faq.question: How do I get to Alunite Ridge from Junction, Utah?
faq.answer: From Junction, drive north on US 89 for about 18 miles toward Salina. Turn east onto Piute County Road 17 and follow it approximately 12 miles as it climbs in elevation. Look for natural pullouts near exposed mineral outcrops on your right. A high-clearance vehicle is recommended but not always required in dry conditions.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Alunite Ridge?
faq.answer: May through September offers the most comfortable collecting weather at this 8,500-foot elevation. Summer days are warm and dry, though afternoons can get hot. Spring (May-June) and fall (September) provide cooler temperatures and are ideal for families. Winter snow can make access difficult.

faq.question: Is Alunite Ridge suitable for beginning rockhounders and kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Alunite Ridge is beginner-friendly and appropriate for kids ages 6 and up. The terrain is relatively open with easy access to specimens along the ridge shoulders and erosional washes. No advanced digging or technical climbing is required, making it perfect for families introducing children to rockhounding.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring to collect at Alunite Ridge?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain, a small sledgehammer or pry bar to separate specimens from host rock, a collection bag, and plenty of water. A magnifying glass helps identify feldspars and other mineral details. Sun protection, a map, and a GPS unit are recommended given the remote location and spotty cell service.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Junction, Utah. Find quartz, feldspar, and mica. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Easy access for families.
